28: 	"github.com/hashicorp/terraform/internal/tfdiags"
29: )
30: 
31: // NodeAbstractResourceInstance represents a resource instance with no
32: // associated operations. It embeds NodeAbstractResource but additionally
33: // contains an instance key, used to identify one of potentially many
34: // instances that were created from a resource in configuration, e.g. using
35: // the "count" or "for_each" arguments.
36: type NodeAbstractResourceInstance struct {
37: 	NodeAbstractResource
38: 	Addr addrs.AbsResourceInstance
39: 
40: 	// These are set via the AttachState method.
41: 	instanceState *states.ResourceInstance
42: 
43: 	Dependencies []addrs.ConfigResource
44: 
45: 	preDestroyRefresh bool
46: 
47: 	// During import we may generate configuration for a resource, which needs
48: 	// to be stored in the final change.
49: 	generatedConfigHCL string
50: 
51: 	// override is set by the graph itself, just before this node executes.
52: 	override *configs.Override
53: }
54: 
55: // NewNodeAbstractResourceInstance creates an abstract resource instance graph
56: // node for the given absolute resource instance address.
57: func NewNodeAbstractResourceInstance(addr addrs.AbsResourceInstance) *NodeAbstractResourceInstance {
58: 	// Due to the fact that we embed NodeAbstractResource, the given address
59: 	// actually ends up split between the resource address in the embedded
60: 	// object and the InstanceKey field in our own struct. The
61: 	// ResourceInstanceAddr method will stick these back together again on
62: 	// request.
63: 	r := NewNodeAbstractResource(addr.ContainingResource().Config())
64: 	return &NodeAbstractResourceInstance{
65: 		NodeAbstractResource: *r,
66: 		Addr:                 addr,
67: 	}
68: }
69: 
70: func (n *NodeAbstractResourceInstance) Name() string {
71: 	return n.ResourceInstanceAddr().String()
72: }
73: 
74: func (n *NodeAbstractResourceInstance) Path() addrs.ModuleInstance {
75: 	return n.Addr.Module
76: }
77: 
78: func (n *NodeAbstractResourceInstance) HookResourceIdentity() HookResourceIdentity {
79: 	return HookResourceIdentity{
80: 		Addr:         n.Addr,
81: 		ProviderAddr: n.ResolvedProvider.Provider,
82: 	}
83: }
84: 
85: // GraphNodeReferenceable
86: func (n *NodeAbstractResourceInstance) ReferenceableAddrs() []addrs.Referenceable {
87: 	addr := n.ResourceInstanceAddr()
88: 	return []addrs.Referenceable{
89: 		addr.Resource,
90: 
91: 		// A resource instance can also be referenced by the address of its
92: 		// containing resource, so that e.g. a reference to aws_instance.foo
93: 		// would match both aws_instance.foo[0] and aws_instance.foo[1].
94: 		addr.ContainingResource().Resource,
95: 	}
96: }
97: 
98: // GraphNodeReferencer
99: func (n *NodeAbstractResourceInstance) References() []*addrs.Reference {
100: 	// If we have a configuration attached then we'll delegate to our
101: 	// embedded abstract resource, which knows how to extract dependencies
102: 	// from configuration. If there is no config, then the dependencies will
103: 	// be connected during destroy from those stored in the state.
104: 	if n.Config != nil {
105: 		if n.Schema == nil {
106: 			// We'll produce a log message about this out here so that
107: 			// we can include the full instance address, since the equivalent
108: 			// message in NodeAbstractResource.References cannot see it.
109: 			log.Printf("[WARN] no schema is attached to %s, so config references cannot be detected", n.Name())
110: 			return nil
111: 		}
112: 		return n.NodeAbstractResource.References()
113: 	}
114: 
115: 	// If we have neither config nor state then we have no references.
116: 	return nil
117: }
118: 
119: // StateDependencies returns the dependencies which will be saved in the state
120: // for managed resources, or the most current dependencies for data resources.
121: func (n *NodeAbstractResourceInstance) StateDependencies() []addrs.ConfigResource {
122: 	// Managed resources prefer the stored dependencies, to avoid possible
123: 	// conflicts in ordering when refactoring configuration.
124: 	if s := n.instanceState; s != nil {
125: 		if s.Current != nil {
126: 			return s.Current.Dependencies
127: 		}
128: 	}
129: 
130: 	// If there are no stored dependencies, this is either a newly created
131: 	// managed resource, or a data source, and we can use the most recently
132: 	// calculated dependencies.
133: 	return n.Dependencies
134: }
135: 
136: // GraphNodeResourceInstance
137: func (n *NodeAbstractResourceInstance) ResourceInstanceAddr() addrs.AbsResourceInstance {
138: 	return n.Addr
139: }
140: 
141: // GraphNodeAttachResourceState
142: func (n *NodeAbstractResourceInstance) AttachResourceState(s *states.Resource) {
143: 	if s == nil {
144: 		log.Printf("[WARN] attaching nil state to %s", n.Addr)
145: 		return
146: 	}
147: 	log.Printf("[TRACE] NodeAbstractResourceInstance.AttachResourceState for %s", n.Addr)
148: 	n.instanceState = s.Instance(n.Addr.Resource.Key)
149: 	n.storedProviderConfig = s.ProviderConfig
150: }
151: 
152: // GraphNodeOverridable
153: func (n *NodeAbstractResourceInstance) ConfigProvider() addrs.AbsProviderConfig {
154: 	return n.ResolvedProvider
155: }
156: 
157: // GraphNodeOverridable
158: func (n *NodeAbstractResourceInstance) SetOverride(override *configs.Override) {
159: 	n.override = override
160: }
161: 
162: // readDiff returns the planned change for a particular resource instance
163: // object.
164: func (n *NodeAbstractResourceInstance) readDiff(ctx EvalContext, providerSchema providers.ProviderSchema) (*plans.ResourceInstanceChange, error) {
165: 	changes := ctx.Changes()
166: 	addr := n.ResourceInstanceAddr()
167: 
168: 	schema, _ := providerSchema.SchemaForResourceAddr(addr.Resource.Resource)
169: 	if schema == nil {
170: 		// Should be caught during validation, so we don't bother with a pretty error here
171: 		return nil, fmt.Errorf("provider does not support resource type %q", addr.Resource.Resource.Type)
172: 	}
173: 
174: 	change := changes.GetResourceInstanceChange(addr, addrs.NotDeposed)
175: 
176: 	log.Printf("[TRACE] readDiff: Read %s change from plan for %s", change.Action, n.Addr)
177: 
178: 	return change, nil
179: }
180: 
181: func (n *NodeAbstractResourceInstance) checkPreventDestroy(change *plans.ResourceInstanceChange) tfdiags.Diagnostics {
182: 	if change == nil || n.Config == nil || n.Config.Managed == nil {
183: 		return nil
184: 	}
185: 
186: 	preventDestroy := n.Config.Managed.PreventDestroy
187: 
188: 	if (change.Action == plans.Delete || change.Action.IsReplace()) && preventDestroy {
189: 		var diags tfdiags.Diagnostics
190: 		diags = diags.Append(&hcl.Diagnostic{
191: 			Severity: hcl.DiagError,
192: 			Summary:  "Instance cannot be destroyed",
193: 			Detail: fmt.Sprintf(
194: 				"Resource %s has lifecycle.prevent_destroy set, but the plan calls for this resource to be destroyed. To avoid this error and continue with the plan, either disable lifecycle.prevent_destroy or reduce the scope of the plan using the -target option.",
195: 				n.Addr.String(),
196: 			),
197: 			Subject: &n.Config.DeclRange,
198: 		})
199: 		return diags
200: 	}
